title: Intercostobrachial nerve (post-ALND sensory loss)
topic: Nerves at risk in axillary surgery
vignette: Woman a month after mastectomy + axillary lymph node dissection has burning/aching and decreased sensation of the medial upper arm; normal shoulder ROM, no weakness. Injured nerve?

clues.note: Intercostobrachial nerve is the most frequently injured nerve in ALND.
No motor deficit -> rules out motor nerves.

whatMatters: Pure sensory deficit of the medial upper arm after ALND = intercostobrachial nerve (T2).
strategy: Classify deficit as sensory vs motor first. Burning/numbness = sensory nerve; weakness/winging = motor nerve.

eliminations.why: No cutaneous sensation; no weakness here.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had weakness with arm adduction rather than sensory loss in the axilla/medial arm

eliminations.why: Doesn't traverse axilla; causes weak abduction/ER.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had weakness of shoulder abduction and external rotation (suprascapular nerve)

eliminations.why: Purely motor; can't cause sensory loss.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had weakness of shoulder extension/adduction without any sensory loss (thoracodorsal nerve)

reasoning: Normal ROM rules out motor nerves
Territory = medial upper arm/axilla after ALND
Pure sensory medial-arm nerve = intercostobrachial -> A
mechanism: The intercostobrachial nerve (lateral cutaneous branch of T2) is purely sensory to the axilla and medial upper arm and crosses the surgical field in ALND, the most commonly injured nerve there.

firstAid.topic: Nerves at risk in breast/axillary surgery

memoryHook: Match modality before fame — sensory deficit = sensory nerve, not the famous motor one.
